Non-arc episodes of _X-Files_ aren't filler, by the way.  They're the main event, the vast majority of the content.

replies.text:
I don't completely disagree with including 'The X-Files' as the first two (or three?) seasons were very good, but later seasons went down the 'everything is a deep state conspiracy' stuff and was boring to the point I stopped watching.

Two TV shows that I have always wonder why no YouTube critics covered were 'Grimm' and 'Resident Alien'.

Grimm in my view was perfect from start to finish, the last season was only half the episodes of all the other seasons , because that is all the time they needed to get to an end conclusion. Plus it had so many exceptional actors in it, allowing for humour that is natural between people who know each other well.

Resident Alien has two excellent seasons and I am hoping the next one maintains the smart and dark comedy of S1 & S2. Alan Tudyk is perfectly cast as the Alien and this is one of those shows you want pre-recorded so that you can skip back a few seconds to catch what you missed laughing so much.

In October 2018 I stop watching broadcast TV & cancelled my Sky TV subscription, I used the money saved not giving the slime/deviant/vermin at the BBC a penny in the form of the TV-TAX.  Back then the price was about to go up £13.10 a month, Sky was also about to increase the monthly charge to £27. When cancelling I was offered a price cut to £12pm, but I told the guy that even if they gave it to me for free it would be too much, as I would have to pay the TV-TAX legally  use it.

The TV-TAX and Sky were soon to come £40 a month or £480 a year. I started buying DVDs, CDs for music along with a few Blue-ray discs. The reality is that I have have spent maybe half of that money saved and now have 1000s of hours of TV, Anime and Movies that I enjoy that are also always accessible in seconds.

The very first Box-Sets I purchased were Stargate SG-1 and Atlantis, followed by Grim, the original Start Treks and Elementary. Those five box-sets (plus the original Stargate movie were not cheap as they cost me a little over £300, but compared to the Sky+TV-TAX at £480 per year they were a bargain. SG-1 alone has over 200 episodes and Atlantis has 100+, meaning I could watch 1 per day for an entire year.

More recently I have added ''The Sweeny', 'Rising Damp', ‘Dinner Ladies ’, 'I Claudious', 'Cadfile' and more, buying DVDs means I never have e to binge watch a show because it will be leaving Netflix soon, and re-watching is free (excluding the electricity) any time I like.

replies.author: @ainslieberrafella
replies.text:
The X-Files was very successful but ultimately disappointing, because it was written in a way to keep the viewers hooked and constantly hoping for a pay-off that simply never arrived. They even gaslit viewers by telling them, 'The Truth is Out There' while never actually delivering a satisfying ending. Don't get me wrong, just like millions of others I loved The X-Files, but in retrospect the writing was highly deceptive. This style of writing, where plot solutions are suggested but never actualised,  was further capitalised by writers like Damon Lindelof on Lost, which was another immensely successful series that kept viewers on tenterhooks for years, desperately hoping to find out What Happened? But, in the end it was all a con, and it's purpose was never to tell a story, but to keep the viewers hooked and hoping. 
Incidentally, when Lost came out, I watched about three episodes and recognised this similarity with The X-Files and gave up on it right away. I'm pretty sure I didn't miss much.

replies.author: @violetmoon2283
replies.text: @buffaloandtrial When they had two weeks to work on it, a fraction of the budget, and there are 20 other episodes in the season, yes it can be charming seeing them try new things and experiment a bit. It's less charming when they've had 2 year to work on it, a massively inflated budget, and there are only 8 episodes in the season. They have artificially inflated the stakes by slimming seasons down so much and having everything be one overarching story instead of a much longer season that has room to dilly dally and experiment a bit. The dilly dallying and experimenting is where a lot of the good world building and character exploration used to happen. It all has to happen in narrative now, and more often then not, they don't do a very good job of it.

replies.author: @buffaloandtrial
replies.text:
@violetmoon2283I agree with a large part of what you’re saying. But, again, what’s the prognosis? Just going back to the same model and that’s it, we consider the creative process a solved problem? I think each story/narrative/idea should be treated according to its merit. Some ideas deserve a well made handful of episodes (instead of cramming or extending everything into 8 episodes), others deserve more of the model you’re describing. I personally cannot stand filler episodes in a 20+ episode season, but I also find filler in 8 episode seasons to be inexcusable. But I respect those who do enjoy filler. 

I don’t know much about the industry, so I can’t comment much on that side of things, but it is unfortunate ideas are forced into one model or the other. Yes it’s true that have limitations can bring out creativity, but it’s clear to me whoever is imposing these limitations and expectations (likely the corporate chairs) are suffocating the process.

replies.author: @merikano2985
replies.text: The momentum is the biggest problem. I've been writing a fanfic that began 11 months ago. It has more words than all of Harry Potter because I stuck to putting out a chapter every week or every two weeks. And readers are invested! But I know if I take my foot off the gas and let it coast a few months, that the interest and momentum I have now will be lost. There's an unspoken trust between creators and people who spend hours of their lives watching or reading your work. And it's this: "give us something." It doesn't have to be the quality of Stephen Spielberg or Steven King. Not everyone is a modern day Shakespeare. But there's a reason we keep coming back to your slop every weekend. Like one of those divorced dads who loves his kids and wants to be there for them, for better or worse, he'll be there. He won't be a deadbeat that let's them down. Which is what Hollywood has largely become.

replies.author: @AEB1066
replies.text:
A lot of the problem is that TV producers like to think that they are A-list movie producers these days.

A show I watched when I visit my parents was Vera. Like every other show the episode count fell series after series until the final one had three episodes. They also had a send off documentary special.

They show the filming of one scene - a converstaion between the two main characters walking outside a building. There were three 4k camera crews - one for each actor and another for the distance shot - and a whole convoy of trucks for catering, make up, hair, computers, lights, generators and gods knows what else. So to film a scene that was maybe two minutes of the program needed a day with forty people and an insane amount of equipment.

Yet only a decade of so ago they'd have used a single camera, done each take seperately, had maybe five people in support, and would get the shot done in less than an hour and move on to the next scene.

Until TV and streaming production can get back to being fast and lean the death spiral will continue. As viewership and advertsining dries up programs that need millions per episode to make will fade to vanity projects if they get made at all.

replies.author: @BalmBeach
@eyespy3001
replies.text:
⁠@BalmBeachSetting is just as fundamental in telling a story as character. In fact, you can tell an entire story about a place and time with no characters whatsoever and have it be a complete story. Try telling a story about characters and their experiences without a setting and see how far you get. 

Face it, speaking in absolutes like, “when the story you’re telling relies on CGI, you’re f’d” is ignorant nonsense. If I want to tell a story about class warfare and bigotry amidst the backdrop of an alien invasion in space, you better believe I’d have to rely on CGI to tell that story. replies.author: @42anon427
replies.text:
Unions.  The problem is unions.  The amount of people they have to have milling about during filming is insane, because of unions and union rules.  They can't consolidate filming roles because of unions.  All sorts of operations have to be performed in a specific way...because of unions.  Extension cord in the way?....well, you can't move it yourself, you have to get the union electrician to do it.  And then every time some parameter is met (length of shooting...location)...there's additional pay involved because of union rules.  The long list of people in the credits...a list of union roles.  "Credits" aren't just to be nice...they're a union requirement.  And then all that union fucking around just adds to the time required to film something.

Every planet on the original Star Trek looks the same, because they did all the filming within the "thirty mile zone" (TMZ...and yes, that is what that acronym means) around LA...if they went outside the TMZ, it triggered additional union pay for the crew.

replies.text:
A lot of the problem is shows being consumed in bulk. Lost really changed the game when it came to TV, and I think it was the first show that really made the serialised content Drinker's talking about here the standard for great TV. Every episode brought new questions, new theories, it was peak water cooler discussion, and the general overall mystery of the show was incredibly compelling. Game of Thrones managed a similar trick, with some differences, where people were waiting for every new episode, watching it as soon as possible often with friends or family, because the investment in the show (not just the unknown but multiple stories with beloved characters) was just so high. 
Now it feels like people consume something in bulk, almost begrudgingly, because they feel the need to be in-the-know and validate their fandom of any given franchise.

replies.author: @eyespy3001
replies.text:
@_minzez_2926Ok, but one has nothing to do with the other. What does “doomday” have to do with episodic television writers??? In fact, what _are_ episodic writers? Last I checked, they’re just called writers. There aren’t sub-classes of writers. 

Back to “doomday,” though… They shoot without a finished script because of scheduling. Actors have multiple jobs, and having dozens of actors creates a scheduling clusterfuck. So, they just shoot with whoever is available going off of the story outline they already have. Not having a script is not the same as not having a screenplay. The script is just the words the performers say. The screenplay is everything else; the story, the setting, the directions, and the action. On top of that, most of the performers in “doomday” are wearing some sort of costume and/or mask. You don’t need a script to film them, because the dialogue will be added in post-production. So it seems as if you’re making a mountain out of a molehill here.

replies.author: @Raskolnikov70
replies.text:
@crafty1098 True, but it was positive change. Those direct-to-video knockoffs and sequels still made money for the studios through VHS/DVD rental fees. An entire economic ecosystem developed that made the independent film boom of the 1990's possible. Studios could afford to take chances on unknown filmmakers and movies made for niche audiences because they knew they'd at least get their money back and might just strike gold.

 Once widely available high-speed internet (yarr...) and streaming services came along, the bottom dropped out. Studios could still make money on big-budget action and superhero films that got people into the theaters, but the home video revenue evaporated. author: @gnarwhal7562
text:
Part of the appeal of TV was that it was cheaper to make and aired new seasons annually. Now that the quality has steadily gone up (arguably) thanks to prestige television from HBO and AMC, every TV show's production cycle has become almost as complicated and time consuming as film production. 

What we're left with are shows where new seasons seemingly have the same wait times as the Spider-Verse sequels, and with fewer episodes too. Consistency was TV's main appeal, but now that that's out of the window, all of the momentum previous seasons build up to just sort of....dissipates. 

It also doesn't help that streaming has popularized the "fire-hose technique" of just dropping new episodes all at once. The first week is great for discussion, but then the hype just dies after 2 or 3 weeks. Cable television had its issues, but at least episodes aired weekly, shows had 21-30 episodes per season, and the wait time wasn't literal years.

 Now that that's not the case, YouTube and livestreaming have taken its place to offer that consistency. Until the TV landscape can restore some semblance of the old cable network production model, it'll likely just continue to decline as time marches on

author: @GdSza
text:
It’s been years since I’ve binged on a streaming series for the very factors you cite.
When an episodic series had a bad episode, that was it, bad episodes were isolated here and there.
When a serial series has a bad episode, the whole series goes off the rails and it’s very tough to reset.
When I did watch, I found the serialized series became shaggy dog stories, losing the point and feeling like overbloated soap operas.

author: @shadowdramon01
text:
Expanding on the “these were clearly meant to be movies” point is that, like modern movies, modern tv shows work with overinflated budgets. 

There was a time where big shows either made new stars, like George Clooney on ER or Denzel Washington on St Elsewhere, or cast big stars at, or near, the nadirs of their careers, genre shows had notoriously cheesy effects (like every alien just simply being a human with bad makeup) and shows like Friends and The Big Bang Theory paying million-dollar salaries to all their leads, at the height of the show’s success, was a rarity. 

Now, most shows cast big names in the lead roles, instead of nobodies that can grow into big names, and genre uses the most expensive VFX and shooting locations, and we now see shows that are a third or fourth the length of older ones with budgets meant for, like a romcom or a horror film.  Hell, several romcoms and horror films.

It had the great self-contained episodes and the serialized storytelling and nailed it. Someone also mentioned Supernatural and yeah I'd co-sign that even if I haven't seen it myself. We need more of those.

I think while it was happening for a couple of years before that, Covid ruined their workflow to such an extent that they haven't recovered.

replies.text: They were also cheap to make. No large set peices. No constant CGI. You got a practical effect monster and the occasional visual effects shot of aa spaceship. That was about it. The creativity was in the writing and execution, with competent directors and played be competent actors.

replies.author: @crafty1098
replies.text:
Maybe. I just wrote another comment with another theory. In the 60s-80s, just about everyone was watching the same things, at least in the USA, because we only had four TV channels and no VCR. Then more options became available. 
Now it's so fragmented, niche, and targeted. You get pigeon-holed early and aren't forced to broaden your experiences and tastes. And the creators can't count on the whole audience all having basically the same cultural touchstones. It's either references to old media, or no references at all. That isn't just brand awareness, it's the cultural language we use to talk about life and the world.

Every episode was a new adventure, with different outcomes. You never knew which obstacle or enemy your favorite heroes had to face each week. Now I just get tired with modern shows because whenever new season drops I'm done with it within a week and next season will be in so long I wonder if I'll even be still alive to watch it.

PS. Damn, by now I even forgot Stranger Things was a thing, not to mention the plot of previous seasons.

text:
My two reasons why TV is so much worse now:
1.  Writing rooms are required to write for the Two-Screeners, people who look at their phones while "watching" the show. This split of attention means every plot point needs to be spelled out in obvious bold type so that people can look up from TikTok to understand what just happened and nothing can require the viewer to actually Watch the Fucking Show.
2.  Increased reliance on CGI for Fantasy and Sci-Fi genre shows means prolonged production time just to do the rendering.
